intent: Check whether the candidate can explain OOP pillars through behavior and design trade-offs rather than only definitions.
primaryQuestion: What are the four pillars of OOP: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction?

expectedSignals.label: Defines encapsulation as hiding internal state behind controlled behavior.

requirements.description: Mentions protecting invariants and avoiding direct uncontrolled field mutation.
Uses methods or access modifiers as examples of control.

expectedSignals.label: Defines inheritance as reusing or specializing behavior through an is-a relationship.

requirements.description: Explains base and derived class relationship.
Mentions inheritance should preserve substitutability.

expectedSignals.label: Defines polymorphism as using a common interface with different implementations.

requirements.description: Explains callers can use a base type or interface without knowing concrete class.
Gives an example such as different payment or notification implementations.

expectedSignals.label: Defines abstraction as exposing essential behavior while hiding details.

requirements.description: Mentions interfaces, abstract classes, or public APIs.
Connects abstraction to reducing complexity for callers.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Which pillar is most related to hiding an account balance update behind deposit and withdraw methods?
How can inheritance and polymorphism work together?

en.title: The four pillars of OOP
en.displayQuestion: What are the four pillars of OOP: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can explain OOP pillars through behavior and design trade-offs rather than only definitions.
guidance: Start with the core mechanism, not only a definition.

commonMistakes: Answering only with memorized keywords.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands class as blueprint or constructor concept and object as runtime instance with state and behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between a class and an object, and what is instantiation?

expectedSignals.label: Defines a class as a template or constructor for objects.

requirements.description: Class describes fields, methods, constructor, and behavior shape.
Avoids saying the class itself is the object instance.

expectedSignals.label: Defines an object as a runtime instance with identity and state.

requirements.description: Object has actual property values.
Different instances can have different state while sharing behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Explains instantiation.

requirements.description: Instantiation creates a new object from a class, typically with new in JavaScript or TypeScript.
Constructor arguments initialize state or dependencies.

expectedSignals.label: Connects to JavaScript prototype behavior.

requirements.description: Mentions class methods are shared through prototypes in JavaScript.
Avoids treating JS classes as unrelated to objects/prototypes.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.

Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands extends, prototype-backed inheritance, and why composition or interfaces often replace multiple inheritance.
primaryQuestion: Explain inheritance. How do JavaScript and TypeScript handle single and multiple inheritance?

expectedSignals.label: Defines inheritance as deriving behavior or contract from a base class.

requirements.description: Subclass can reuse or override base behavior.
Inheritance should represent a true is-a relationship.

expectedSignals.label: Explains JavaScript and TypeScript single class inheritance.

requirements.description: JavaScript classes use extends for one base class.
TypeScript follows JavaScript runtime limits for class inheritance.

expectedSignals.label: Explains alternatives to multiple inheritance.

requirements.description: TypeScript classes can implement multiple interfaces.
Composition and mixins can share behavior without deep class inheritance.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions maintenance risks of deep inheritance.

requirements.description: Deep hierarchies make behavior harder to trace.
Base class changes can unexpectedly affect many subclasses.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands encapsulation as protecting invariants and controlling state changes.
primaryQuestion: What is encapsulation, and how do public, private, and protected enforce it in TypeScript?

expectedSignals.label: Defines encapsulation as controlling access to internal state and behavior.

requirements.description: Internal representation can change without breaking callers.
Callers use public methods or API rather than reaching into internals.

expectedSignals.label: Explains TypeScript access modifiers.

requirements.description: public is accessible everywhere by default.
private restricts access within the declaring class at compile time.
protected allows access in subclasses.

expectedSignals.label: Connects encapsulation to invariants.

requirements.description: Methods can validate state transitions before mutation.
Prevents invalid states such as negative balance or inconsistent status.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions TypeScript private versus runtime privacy nuance.

requirements.description: TypeScript private is mainly checked by the compiler.
JavaScript #private fields have runtime privacy semantics.

Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can choose an abstraction form based on shared implementation, runtime needs, and type contract shape.
primaryQuestion: What is abstraction, and when would you use an abstract class versus an interface in TypeScript?

expectedSignals.label: Defines abstraction as exposing essential behavior while hiding implementation details.

requirements.description: Callers rely on a smaller stable contract.
Implementation details can change behind that contract.

expectedSignals.label: Explains interface as a type-level contract.

requirements.description: Interface describes required shape or capabilities.
Interfaces do not emit runtime JavaScript implementation.

expectedSignals.label: Explains abstract class as shared base with possible implementation.

requirements.description: Abstract class can contain implemented methods, state, constructor logic, and abstract methods.
Abstract class exists as runtime JavaScript class output.

expectedSignals.label: Chooses based on design needs.

requirements.description: Uses interface for capability contracts and multiple implementers.
Uses abstract class when sharing base behavior or lifecycle is valuable.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands has-a design, flexible behavior assembly, and inheritance risk.
primaryQuestion: What is composition over inheritance, and why is it often preferred?

expectedSignals.label: Defines composition as building behavior from contained collaborators.

requirements.description: An object has collaborators rather than being a subclass of them.
Behavior can be delegated to injected or owned components.

expectedSignals.label: Explains why composition is flexible.

requirements.description: Can swap or combine behaviors without deep hierarchies.
Avoids inheriting unwanted methods or state.

expectedSignals.label: Explains when inheritance is still useful.

requirements.description: Inheritance is reasonable for a true substitutable is-a relationship.
Shared base behavior can be useful when the hierarchy is stable and small.

expectedSignals.label: Gives a concrete scenario.

requirements.description: Uses examples such as UserService has EmailSender, Car has Engine, or Controller has Service.
Explains why extending would be misleading in that scenario.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can initialize valid objects without hiding heavy side effects or asynchronous work in constructors.
primaryQuestion: What is a constructor, and what responsibilities should and should not be placed inside it?

expectedSignals.label: Defines constructor as initialization logic run when creating an instance.

requirements.description: Constructor receives initial values or dependencies.
It establishes required state for the object.

expectedSignals.label: Places invariant validation and simple assignment appropriately.

requirements.description: Validates required fields or impossible initial state.
Assigns dependencies and initial state without doing unrelated workflows.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids heavy side effects in constructors.

requirements.description: Avoids network calls, database writes, event publishing, or long-running operations in constructors.
Explains that constructors should not hide failure-prone business processes.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions factories or async initialization alternatives.

requirements.description: Uses factory method or service method when creation requires async work.
Keeps object construction predictable and testable.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

en.title: Constructor responsibilities
en.displayQuestion: What is a constructor, and what responsibilities should and should not be placed inside it?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate whether the candidate can initialize valid objects without hiding heavy side effects or asynchronous work in constructors.
guidance: Start with the core mechanism, not only a definition.
Use a concrete backend or frontend example where the concept changes behavior.
Name one common mistake or production consequence.
commonMistakes: Answering only with memorized keywords.
Ignoring boundary cases or failure modes.
Not connecting the concept to real implementation decisions.

vi.title: Trách nhiệm của constructor
vi.displayQuestion: Constructor là gì, và trách nhiệm nào nên hoặc không nên đặt trong constructor?
vi.displayIntent: Evaluate whether the candidate can initialize valid objects without hiding heavy side effects or asynchronous work in constructors.
guidance: Bắt đầu bằng cơ chế cốt lõi, không chỉ đọc định nghĩa.
Dùng một ví dụ backend hoặc frontend cụ thể nơi khái niệm này làm thay đổi hành vi.
Nêu một lỗi phổ biến hoặc hệ quả trong production.
commonMistakes: Chỉ trả lời bằng keyword học thuộc.
Bỏ qua boundary case hoặc failure mode.
Không kết nối khái niệm với quyết định implement thực tế.

ja.title: Constructor responsibilities
ja.displayQuestion: 次の点を説明してください: What is a constructor, and what responsibilities should and should not be placed inside it?
ja.displayIntent: この質問は constructors を実務で説明できるかを確認します。
guidance: 定義だけでなく、中心となる仕組みから説明する。
その概念が挙動を変える具体的な backend/frontend の例を使う。
よくある mistake または production consequence を一つ述べる。
commonMistakes: 暗記した keyword だけで答える。
boundary case や failure mode を無視する。
概念を実装上の判断につなげない。

intent: Check whether the candidate knows where state and behavior live and avoids using static members for per-user or per-request data.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between instance members and static members?

expectedSignals.label: Defines instance members as belonging to each object instance.

requirements.description: Each instance can have its own field values.
Instance methods usually operate on this instance state.

expectedSignals.label: Defines static members as belonging to the class itself.

requirements.description: Static properties and methods are accessed on the class, not a specific instance.
Static methods do not receive instance this unless an instance is passed.

expectedSignals.label: Gives practical use cases.

requirements.description: Static factory/helper methods or constants can be appropriate.
Instance members are appropriate for per-object state and behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions risks of shared static mutable state.

requirements.description: Static mutable state is shared across all users or calls in a process.
It can create test leakage or request cross-talk if misused.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Would currentUser be a good static field on User? Why not?
What is a good example of a static factory method?

intent: Assess whether the candidate can debug lost receiver bugs in class methods and callback-based code.
primaryQuestion: How does this behave inside a class method in JavaScript or TypeScript, and what happens when a method is passed as a callback?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that this in a method depends on the call receiver.

requirements.description: instance.method() sets this to instance.
The method is still a function whose this is determined by how it is called.

expectedSignals.label: Explains detached method callback problem.

requirements.description: Passing instance.method as a callback can lose the receiver.
this may become undefined in strict mode or point somewhere unexpected.

expectedSignals.label: Names practical fixes.

requirements.description: Use bind, wrapper arrow function, class field arrow method, or explicit dependency passing as appropriate.
Chooses fix based on readability and lifecycle.

expectedSignals.label: Connects to event handlers or async callbacks.

requirements.description: Mentions UI event handlers, timers, promises, or framework callbacks as common places this is lost.
Explains why tests should cover callback invocation shape.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Why can button.onClick = user.getName break when clicked later?
What is the difference between passing this.handleClick and () => this.handleClick()?

intent: Check whether the candidate understands compile-time versus runtime privacy and its design implications.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between private in TypeScript and JavaScript #private fields?

expectedSignals.label: Explains TypeScript private as a compile-time access restriction.

requirements.description: TypeScript reports errors when code accesses private members improperly.
The emitted JavaScript may still contain ordinary properties depending on target and configuration.

expectedSignals.label: Explains JavaScript #private as runtime-enforced private fields.

requirements.description: #private fields have language-level access restrictions.
They cannot be accessed with normal bracket or dot property access from outside the class.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions interoperability and tooling implications.

requirements.description: #private syntax affects emitted runtime code and compatibility targets.
TypeScript private can be easier to inspect but less runtime-protected.

expectedSignals.label: Connects privacy to encapsulation rather than secrecy alone.

requirements.description: Private members protect invariants and implementation details.
Sensitive secrets still require security design beyond access modifiers.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Can TypeScript private always stop runtime access after compilation?
Should access modifiers be treated as a security boundary for secrets?

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands TypeScript interface compatibility by shape rather than nominal class identity.
primaryQuestion: What does it mean that TypeScript uses structural typing, and how is it related to duck typing?

expectedSignals.label: Defines structural typing as compatibility by shape.

requirements.description: A value can satisfy an interface if it has the required properties and methods.
Explicit implements is not always required for assignment compatibility.

expectedSignals.label: Relates to duck typing.

requirements.description: If it has the needed behavior shape, callers can use it as that type.
TypeScript checks this statically while JavaScript behavior is runtime.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions benefits.

requirements.description: Encourages flexible contracts and easier testing with object literals or fakes.
Supports interface-based design without nominal inheritance.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions risks.

requirements.description: Accidental compatibility can happen when shapes match unintentionally.
Runtime data still needs validation because TypeScript types disappear at runtime.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Can an object literal satisfy an interface without declaring implements?
Why does structural typing not validate JSON from an API at runtime?

intent: Check whether the candidate can model object relationships without overusing inheritance.
primaryQuestion: What is a has-a relationship versus an is-a relationship, and how do association, aggregation, and composition fit in?

expectedSignals.label: Defines is-a as inheritance or substitutable specialization.

requirements.description: A subclass should be usable wherever the base class is expected.
Uses examples like AdminUser is a User only if contract expectations hold.

expectedSignals.label: Defines has-a as ownership or collaboration through references.

requirements.description: An Order has OrderItems or a UserService has EmailSender.
Uses composition or aggregation rather than extends.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes association, aggregation, and composition at a high level.

requirements.description: Association is a general relationship between objects.
Aggregation is a weaker whole-part relationship where parts can live independently.
Composition is stronger ownership where part lifecycle is tied to the whole.

expectedSignals.label: Connects relationship choice to maintainability.

requirements.description: Wrong is-a modeling leads to LSP issues.
Composition often keeps responsibilities clearer.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Is Order extends User a reasonable model? Why not?
Give an example of composition where the part should not live without the whole.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can reason about identity, equality, and mutation in simple domain models.
primaryQuestion: What is an immutable object, and what is the difference between a value object and an entity in domain modeling?

expectedSignals.label: Defines immutable object.

requirements.description: State cannot be changed after creation through public API.
Changes produce a new object or value instead of mutating existing state.

expectedSignals.label: Explains why immutability helps reasoning.

requirements.description: Reduces hidden side effects and shared mutation bugs.
Makes equality, caching, and tests easier in many cases.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes value object from entity.

requirements.description: Value object identity is based on its values, such as Money or EmailAddress.
Entity identity is stable over time, such as User or Order, even as attributes change.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions practical modeling trade-offs.

requirements.description: Not every object must be immutable.
Entities can contain immutable value objects to protect invariants.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Is an EmailAddress usually a value object or an entity? Why?
Why might a User entity change while a Money value object stays immutable?

intent: Check whether the candidate can protect domain rules through object behavior rather than uncontrolled public state.
primaryQuestion: What is encapsulation of invariants, and why is exposing behavior through methods often better than exposing public fields?

expectedSignals.label: Defines invariant as a rule that must always hold.

requirements.description: Examples include account balance cannot be negative or order cannot ship before payment.
Invariant should hold before and after public operations.

expectedSignals.label: Explains how methods protect invariants.

requirements.description: Methods can validate inputs and state transitions.
Public fields allow callers to bypass rules accidentally.

expectedSignals.label: Gives a concrete example.

requirements.description: Uses withdraw/deposit instead of direct balance assignment.
Uses order.markPaid or order.ship instead of freely setting status fields.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions DTO/entity boundaries.

requirements.description: DTOs may expose data shape, but domain objects should protect behavior and rules.
Persistence mapping should not force public mutable fields everywhere.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: How would you prevent an account balance from becoming negative?
Why might a DTO expose fields while a domain model exposes methods?

vi.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can protect domain rules through object behavior rather than uncontrolled public state.
guidance: Bắt đầu bằng cơ chế cốt lõi, không chỉ đọc định nghĩa.
Dùng một ví dụ backend hoặc frontend cụ thể nơi khái niệm này làm thay đổi hành vi.
Nêu một lỗi phổ biến hoặc hệ quả trong production.
commonMistakes: Chỉ trả lời bằng keyword học thuộc.
Bỏ qua boundary case hoặc failure mode.
Không kết nối khái niệm với quyết định implement thực tế.

ja.title: Encapsulating invariants through behavior
ja.displayQuestion: 次の点を説明してください: What is encapsulation of invariants, and why is exposing behavior through methods often better than exposing public fields?
ja.displayIntent: この質問は invariants を実務で説明できるかを確認します。
guidance: 定義だけでなく、中心となる仕組みから説明する。
その概念が挙動を変える具体的な backend/frontend の例を使う。
よくある mistake または production consequence を一つ述べる。
commonMistakes: 暗記した keyword だけで答える。
boundary case や failure mode を無視する。
概念を実装上の判断につなげない。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Classes
sourceReferences.note: Used for JavaScript class behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Inheritance and the prototype chain
sourceReferences.note: Used for prototype-backed JavaScript object behavior.
